Recovery Services vaults are concentrated in eastus and use the RS0 Standard SKU with infrastructure encryption enabled: because 68.9% of vaults are in eastus, 97.8% use RS0 and vault encryption shows infrastructureencryption Enabled, plan to deploy the new Azure Backup vault in eastus using RS0 and enable infrastructure encryption to meet encryption‑at‑rest requirements and remain consistent with tenant conventions.

Storage redundancy favors geo‑replication: since 23.3% of storage accounts use Standard_GRS and 17.2% use Standard_RAGRS (with additional LRS usage), design backup staging or any required storage for Azure Backup to use Standard_GRS or Standard_RAGRS so backup data is geo‑redundant in a way consistent with existing tenant choices.

Key Vaults have strong key protection defaults (purge protection and long soft‑delete): because 94.4% of vaults have soft delete set to 90 days and enablepurgeprotection is True for 100% of key vaults, store any customer‑managed keys for Azure Backup in a Key Vault configured with purge protection and a 90‑day soft delete retention to align with tenant recovery and compliance practices.

Managed identities are widely used, often as user‑assigned: because 55% of storage accounts have UserAssigned identities and many compute and platform resources use SystemAssigned, UserAssigned combinations, plan to provision a user‑assigned managed identity for the backup vault/automation to access Key Vault (CMK) and on‑prem integration points for predictable permissions and lifecycle management.

Existing automation tooling is available for orchestration and scheduled tasks: with 95 logic workflows, 10 automation runbooks and several deployment scripts present in the tenant, implement the monthly DR test orchestration using existing Logic Apps or Automation Accounts (reusing or extending current runbooks) to automate snapshot/restore verification, reporting and alerting.

There is substantial on‑prem connectivity capacity to leverage for backups and DR tests: with 170 ExpressRoute cross‑connections across the tenant, design backup replication and monthly DR test traffic to use ExpressRoute or the existing VPN/Direct connections where possible and plan private endpoints or network rules for the vault/Key Vault to secure traffic across that connectivity.

Public network access is commonly enabled on vaults and key resources today: because most Key Vaults and Recovery Services vaults show publicnetworkaccess Enabled, define explicit network controls (private endpoints, service endpoints or allowlists for on‑prem ranges) for the new backup solution so encryption keys and backup operations are secured while accounting for current connectivity patterns.
